content.text: Technical debt that lives only in a register never gets paid. The register is the map; sprint and PI planning are the mechanism that converts that map into scheduled engineering work. Without explicit allocation, debt repayment loses every prioritization argument against feature work — because feature work has a named stakeholder asking for it, and debt repayment does not.

attrs.alt: Sprint capacity allocation diagram showing healthy vs debt-spike sprints, and a 6-sprint PI planning view with front-loaded S1 debt clearance and an IP Sprint for backlog work

content.text: The Allocation Problem

Feature pressure in GenAI teams is intense: model improvements move fast, competitors ship frequently, and stakeholders can always name a capability gap. Without a protected budget, debt repayment is perpetually deferred to "the next sprint" — which never comes.
The trap:

"We'll pay down this debt after the next feature launch." Every feature launch creates new debt. If the debt repayment budget is always zero, debt grows monotonically until an incident forces an unplanned repayment at 3× the cost.
The solution is to treat the debt repayment allocation as a fixed commitment, not a residual. It is decided at PI planning, not negotiated each sprint.
Setting the Allocation Percentage
The right allocation depends on the current state of the debt register. The baseline rule is 15–20% of engineering capacity, but it should scale with debt health:

The 35% recommendation when S1 items are open is not punitive — it reflects that S1 debt (safety risk, uncapped costs, no eval coverage in CI) is a liability that grows every day it remains unresolved.
Sprint-Level Debt Planning
Capacity Calculation
Before debt items can be selected for a sprint, available capacity must be calculated. Capacity is not the same as headcount × sprint days.
Item Selection Algorithm

Once the debt budget is calculated, items are selected from the register. The selection algorithm reflects the severity ordering: S1 items always go first, then S2 items sorted by effort-to-impact ratio.

The rule that S1 items are included even if they exceed the budget reflects the severity definition: an S1 item (active safety risk, uncapped cost) cannot wait for the next sprint simply because the budget was already consumed.
PI Planning — Setting the Quarter's Debt Budget

Program Increment (PI) planning is where the debt budget for an entire quarter is established. This is the appropriate level to make allocation commitments, because debt items are rarely completed in a single sprint and the register needs to be worked as a whole.
Pre-PI Planning: Debt Register Snapshot
Two weeks before PI planning, the engineering lead produces a debt register snapshot that gives leadership a clear picture of what is owed:

## Debt Register Snapshot — Pre-PI Planning (2026-Q3)
**Produced:** 2026-06-18
**Engineering lead:** @eng-lead

### Register summary
| Severity | Count | Total effort | Cumulative interest (per week) |
|----------|-------|--------------|-------------------------------|
| S1 | 2 | 3.5 days | Critical — active risk |
| S2 | 9 | 24 days | High — silent regression risk |
| S3 | 14 | 31 days | Medium — efficiency drag |
| S4 | 7 | 18 days | Low — monitor only |
| **Total** | **32** | **76.5 days** | |

### Recommendation for Q3
- Clear all S1 items in Sprint 1 (3.5d)
- Allocate 20% of capacity (~4d/sprint) for S2 pay-down
- Target: 0 S1 items and < 5 S2 items entering Q4
- Required: 20 engineering-days of debt capacity across Q3

### Impact if not allocated
- Stale RAG index (TD-040) will serve deleted-document content for 40+ users/day
- No eval coverage gap (TD-039) means the next prompt change may degrade table
  extraction silently — discovered only after enterprise escalation
- API key exposure (TD-041) is an active compliance risk that grows each week
PI Planning: Debt Allocation Decision
At PI planning, the debt budget is a first-class agenda item — not an afterthought. The engineering lead presents the snapshot, proposes an allocation, and gets explicit leadership commitment.
PI allocation model
Debt days per sprint
Total Q3 debt days
When to use
Conservative (healthy register)
3d per sprint
18d over 6 sprints
0 S1 items, < 5 S2 items
Standard (normal state)
4d per sprint
24d over 6 sprints
0–2 S1, 5–10 S2 items
Elevated (S1 backlog)
6d in S1, 4d S2–S5
26d total
2+ S1 items entering PI
Recovery (compounding debt)
30% of all sprint capacity
36d over 6 sprints
Debt opened > closed for 2+ quarters

The elevated model front-loads S1 clearance into Sprint 1, then drops to standard pace. This is the pattern shown in the diagram: the team takes a temporary feature velocity hit to eliminate safety and reliability risk before building new capabilities on top of it.
IP Sprint: The Debt Clearance Sprint

Many teams using SAFe or a similar PI planning framework reserve the final sprint of a PI as an "Innovation and Planning" (IP) sprint with reduced feature commitments. This sprint is the natural home for S2 and S3 debt items that could not fit into regular sprints.

def plan_ip_sprint_debt(
    register: list[dict],
    ip_sprint_days: int = 10,
    team_size: int = 6,
    overhead_pct: float = 0.25,   # IP sprint has more planning overhead
) -> dict:
    """
    Plans the debt component of an IP sprint.
    IP sprints typically allocate 40–60% to debt/tech improvements.
    """
    available = ip_sprint_days * team_size * (1 - overhead_pct)
    debt_budget = available * 0.50    # 50% of IP sprint to debt

    s2_items = [i for i in register if i["severity"] == "S2" and not i.get("sprint")]
    s3_items = [i for i in register if i["severity"] == "S3" and not i.get("sprint")]

    selected, remaining = [], debt_budget
    for item in s2_items + s3_items:
        if item["effort_days"] <= remaining:
            selected.append(item)
            remaining -= item["effort_days"]

    # Remaining capacity goes to eval suite expansion and exploratory work
    eval_expansion_days = min(remaining, available * 0.25)

    return {
        "debt_items": selected,
        "debt_days": debt_budget - remaining,
        "eval_expansion_days": eval_expansion_days,
        "total_available_days": available,
    }
The IP sprint is also the right time to run the debt audit that identifies new items to enter the register for the next PI. The audit feeds the next PI planning session with fresh data.
Making Debt Visible to Stakeholders

The biggest obstacle to debt allocation is that stakeholders cannot see debt the way they can see features. A feature has a demo; debt repayment does not. Making debt visible requires translating technical risk into business impact.

## How to Present Debt to Non-Technical Stakeholders

### Instead of:
"We need to spend 4 days on TD-039 — adding eval coverage for multi-page PDF tables."

### Say:
"The document QA feature currently has a blind spot: we have no automated test
coverage for the case where a table spans page boundaries in a PDF. The last time
we shipped a change that affected this scenario, we discovered the regression
from an enterprise support escalation — not from our own monitoring.

Fixing this costs 3 days. Continuing without it means:
- The next prompt change may degrade table extraction for ~200 enterprise users
- We'll discover it from a support ticket, not a dashboard
- The fix will be reactive (post-complaint) rather than proactive

This is the difference between a 3-day proactive investment now and a 3-day
reactive fix plus a 2-week customer trust repair cycle if the regression ships."

The pattern is:  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'state the gap → quantify the blast radius → compare proactive vs. reactive cost', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} . Stakeholders can evaluate a business trade-off; they cannot evaluate a code quality argument.
Tracking Debt Velocity
Debt velocity — the ratio of items opened to items closed per quarter — is the leading indicator of whether the allocation is sufficient.

def compute_debt_velocity(
    register_history: list[dict],
    quarter: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Computes debt velocity for a quarter.
    A velocity < 1.0 means debt is accumulating.
    """
    opened = [i for i in register_history if i["quarter_opened"] == quarter]
    closed = [i for i in register_history if i.get("quarter_closed") == quarter]

    velocity = len(closed) / max(len(opened), 1)
    s1_cleared_pct = (
        len([i for i in closed if i["severity"] == "S1"])
        / max(len([i for i in opened if i["severity"] == "S1"]), 1)
    )

    return {
        "quarter": quarter,
        "opened": len(opened),
        "closed": len(closed),
        "velocity": round(velocity, 2),
        "s1_cleared_pct": round(s1_cleared_pct * 100, 1),
        "status": (
            "healthy" if velocity >= 1.0 and s1_cleared_pct == 100
            else "warning" if velocity >= 0.8
            else "critical"
        ),
    }

# A healthy quarter: closed ≥ opened, all S1 items cleared
result = compute_debt_velocity(register_history, quarter="2026-Q3")
print(f"Debt velocity: {result['velocity']} ({result['status']})")
print(f"S1 cleared: {result['s1_cleared_pct']}%")

A velocity below 1.0 for two consecutive quarters is an escalation signal. It means the team is falling further behind on debt repayment, and the allocation must increase or the feature roadmap must be reduced.
Common Allocation Failure Modes
Failure mode
How it manifests
Correction
Zero allocation default
Debt is discussed but never put in a sprint; 'we'll get to it next quarter'
Make allocation percentage a PI planning input — not a residual
Allocation exists but items are bumped
Sprint planning bumps debt items when a feature PR takes longer than estimated
S1 items are never bumpable; S2 items can only be deferred with PM approval
Debt item granularity too large
5-day debt items cannot fit in sprint alongside feature work
Break items > 3 days into sub-tasks; each sub-task is independently closeable
No business translation
Stakeholders override allocation because they cannot see the value
Use blast-radius + proactive vs. reactive cost framing in every planning meeting
Velocity not tracked
Team believes debt is under control but register is growing
Dashboard showing items opened vs. closed per quarter, visible at PI reviews
Summary
Debt allocation in sprint and PI planning is the mechanism that converts the debt register into paid-down debt:
Fixed allocation, not residual
— decide the percentage at PI planning; do not negotiate it each sprint
Scale with health
— 15% when healthy, 20% standard, 35% when S1 items are open
S1 items override budget
— safety and uncapped-cost items cannot be deferred for capacity reasons
IP Sprint is the S2/S3 clearance mechanism
— 40–50% of IP sprint capacity goes to debt
Translate for stakeholders
— gap + blast radius + proactive vs. reactive cost; not technical quality arguments
Track velocity
— items closed / items opened per quarter; below 1.0 for two quarters triggers escalation
Break large items
— items > 3 days must be sub-tasked so they fit in sprints alongside feature work
